All right instant traffic hacks Facebook ads images, okay? So how to master the most crucial part of your Facebook ad Now this is big stuff guys if you run in Facebook ads you got to understand the images It's I'd say 80 90% of the success of your ad has to do with the image Images are everything Okay, we live in a virtual in a visual world. I mean think about what's hot right now Pinterest Instagram It's all visual right it's all about photos. It's about it's about visual capture. We're a highly distracted world Right now. I mean it it's hard to keep someone's attention for two seconds on your page when they land on your page You've got what two two seconds a fraction of a second two seconds for them to determine whether or not your your page is even Relevant to them before they bounce off and go look for something else same with a Facebook ad I bet that it's a lot faster than two seconds They're not going to be looking around for two whole seconds at your ad In fact, if it doesn't capture the corner of their eye you might not capture them So you didn't even get a direct look right so The other the other reason images is so important is it the pictures can convey More than words. I mean you've always heard the phrase pictures worth a thousand words Well, it is think about trying to describe a picture will take quite a few words, right? So if you've got an image that's conveying The idea that you want to convey it's it's a super powerful part of your ad Okay, so the question is which image to use There's a thousand images out there. You can create your own I'll even show you how Facebook helps you out and lets you choose from a library of images They've actually teamed up with With a stock photo. I can't remember which one but stock photos so you can you can actually just choose from stock photos Choose your favorite image. Obviously the problem there is you can't I mean you have limitations there But choose your own photos choose Facebook photos that The Facebook's got available for you But you need to know which image to use that's what we're gonna go into so a few things to think about Does your image speak to your audience? Okay, you might have a really exciting captivating image that is a Shot straight out of a video game right some monster being Shot out by a robot high explosives something like that and that might be very visually Captivating but if you're trying to tell it sell a supplement to a mature audience that's not gonna work They're not interested they they've trained themselves to gloss over those images and look for something that's relevant to them So make sure your image is speaking to your audience. So think about that. I'll tell you a funny little story about images speaking to your audience I Was running a running an ad here For for a supplement that that's to a mature audience thinking You know just trying to think outside of the box what images would would speak to a you know a mature Audience and I thought well, you know my grandpa likes to take walks in the park Right and he likes to take walks in the park and sit at the bench and and look at the ducks And so there was this image of this mallard duck. It's just this big mallard duck on a white background And I used it and it actually converted really well Really surprising but it worked right so it spoke to my audience it captured their eye so The other question is does it pop right does there's the image pop now that the test that I do with this is I Gotta notice yourself right so when you are On Facebook or Pinterest or Instagram or any image Based social media platform and you're scrolling around since that's what we do on Facebook We go to Facebook Slouching our chair and just start to scroll until something captures our eye or at least the corner of our eye At which point we stop and then see what that is right? So the image needs to pass that test so Notice yourself next time you go to Facebook as soon as you're done listening to this and you hop over to Facebook to check Your what your friends are up to notice? Which images are capturing your eye which post did you stop to read and why so start paying attention to that? Is it because you always stop on the pictures? Where some there's a face and the face is looking at you so you get some eye contact with this image? Is it because it had some bright border? Is it because it was of something? interesting or something You know some topic here. So what is it that made you stop what is it needs to? Your image needs to pass the corner of the eye test, okay? So think about that so next is the sizing of your images now this This is a really interesting topic because Facebook you know as a platform people are gonna be uploading images to Facebook left and right for all kinds of different sizes So Facebook has to make a decision somewhere as to what what size it's gonna optimize for and things like that so The sizing of your image is important the width of the image Needs to be one point nine one times the height, okay? So that's the ratio So almost double the width this height, but not quite one point nine one Width to height so the full-size minimum image sizes are listed here For a mobile device mobile actually has the largest image size on Facebook at 560 by 292 desktop by 400 by 209 and sidebars 100 by 72 So depending on where your ads gonna show These are all the same ratio. So I would definitely just optimize for the biggest one. So grab an Image make sure your images are at least 560 by 292 then you're safe if you if you want the large image look on your ad right now talk about that in a second So that's just an easy rule of thumb make sure your image is 560 by 292 if you want that big image look Obviously you can go higher resolution than this you know more pixels just keep this ratio one point nine one So so keep that in mind just save that number right there on your desktop or in your head or whatever if you don't do that If you don't have at least This is the minimum right here because it's because the sidebar is just little tiny images, right? So if your images and at least 400 by 209 Facebook will actually resize it to a 154 by 154 or if it's even too small to do that It'll resize it to a 90 by 90 now. That's a big difference on the news feed I'll show you the difference of what that looks like here in a second Like I'll show you right now. So here's Facebook All right, so here's the full image. Here's the full 400 400 by 209 image right right there So we're gonna keep coming down here and here we have an image. That's not it's the 154 So this image was the wrong dimension wrong size and it got shrunk to this little box and The copy came here on the right-hand side instead of being underneath so Everywhere, you know Facebook gurus left and right are telling you make sure it's 400 by 209 as a minimum Or that's sort of resize small now Is that size really the best right so first thing you want to do is you want to do an eye test? So what I actually did when running Facebook ads Shrulling through here. I actually found that these are again. These are all the full ones here I'm just scrolling through my personal Facebook account I actually started to notice the small images better because these ones got drowned out to me It's just like big image big image. Oh then this little tiny one You know, especially if it's a good image at this one's obviously gray on gray probably not the hottest image But about a nice captivating small image for whatever reason captured my eye because it had text right next to it I don't know. I liked this little combo. It comes in a clean little box Has the copy right to the right of it So I decided to split test. I didn't know if anybody I didn't know of anyone who would split Test everyone had just told me always go big right? So since I found myself gravitating more towards that smaller box. I created the exact same image in the full size and in a small size so it got resized to the 154 and What actually happened you want to see the results I'll show you the results The results the smaller image actually got twice the click-through rate and Cost half as much as getting a clip on getting a click to my website And then the big image now I'm not saying that this is always the case all I'm saying is don't be afraid to test Run both and see which one works. You might be surprised the image. I was using of course had a lot of color and I think on the smaller scale with it with the copy Tucked up right next to it. I think it made more sense and so I did it the smaller and goodness sake look at the results here, so For virtually the same amount of ad spend I guess it was a little bit less down here, but 16 cents got me a website click here with a large image it cost 30 cents to get me a website click if you look at the Click-through rates over here Again, it was it was more than doubly successful the small image people would click on it more likely than the big one So come to Facebook again and try to notice yourself. Which ones you like better? Are you liking the I'll show you another example of the small versus large image. Those are some collages and stuff Where's another small one see everyone's going big images, but if you get a good strong small one This I don't know why but it worked for me. So definitely make sure to test it. Okay Okay, so now let's talk about some tips on Your image if there's writing on your image if there's any text It needs to be 20% or less of the image if not Facebook actually won't approve the ad Now Facebook has to approve a lot of ads really fast so they might approve the ad, but they'll take it down within 24 hours, so Make sure that's not the case When you're using people in your images Try using faces Again, you use all kinds of stuff I want you guys to test because your market your niche your audience might be different than mine So but I'll give you some tips on what's working for me Okay, so when using people try using faces and when using faces try using emotion and Or eye contact so the side of a person's face Is one thing but eyes looking straight at you look here somebody who's an eyes looking straight at you it captures you it passes the corner Corner the eye test, okay The next thing is try using pattern interrupting images or I mean Facebook is just image loaded that's what it's all about now So try something different try like I said, I've all staring right at the person If it's a maybe it's some attractive, you know, male or female some attractive person on there Well, if you get eye contact with that attractive image It speaks to you, right? So try that try colorful images, there's bright colors and there's dull colors, you know I'm not liking any of these ads right here in this feed Of all these ads this one's showing up the most of me in the corner of my eye This because it's got color and that's that's even a cool color. I've a warm color. We're probably Shock me more. Well, look at that color. That was bright popped right out now instantly this switch. I saw the yellow I saw the red. Okay, so use that corner of the eye test for for your images Now one thing that people have been doing and you can try this Is rotate your image so it's not straight on it's kind of kind of crooked See if you can twist it a little bit in your image editing software Everything so squared up and lined up perfectly and perfect lines that when something is Crooked the text or something all of a sudden your your eye goes to that because it's out of place. Okay, so that's something to try Next thing is strong borders, you'll notice people testing out borders red borders orange borders yellow borders around your image Just to give it more of a pop you can test that Familiar things whether it's a baby or a cat or a duck in the park things that are familiar to your audience I might work while they're gravitate to arms things that things that are ridiculous, right? So a Cat riding a tricycle people are gonna stop and look at that image. What's going on? Or something that's loud so those are some tips try to think outside of the box with your images so you get noticed Now I want to show you a couple things here about About the images.
